explanation: The statement says that whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 should be true at the same time. In LTL, we can express this as: globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is also true.

The sentence indicates that whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' should hold at the same time. In LTL, this can be expressed using the globally operator, meaning that at every point in time, if 'prop_0' is true then 'prop_1' is true.
The statement says that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 should hold at the same time. This is best captured by the LTL formula: globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 also holds.
The phrase "When prop_0, prop_1" indicates that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that time. In LTL, this is expressed as 'Globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 also holds.'
Whenever prop_0 holds, it should be followed (now or in the future) by a situation where prop_1 holds. This is commonly formalized as: always, if prop_0 occurs, then eventually prop_1 must occur.
Whenever prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 should also be true at the same time or after. This can be captured by the implication: globally, if prop_0 holds, then eventually prop_1 must hold.
This translates to: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must hold at the same moment. This is expressed as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.

The statement 'When prop_0, the prop_1' can be interpreted as: whenever prop_0 holds at a given moment, prop_1 should also hold at that same moment—in other words, prop_1 is required whenever prop_0 occurs. This is a safety property, meaning prop_0 -> prop_1 should hold globally.

The statement 'When prop_0, prop_1' suggests that whenever (at any point) prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that point. In LTL, this is expressed as 'globally, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 is also true'.

Whenever prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 should also hold at the same time. This can be captured with 'globally (prop_0 implies prop_1)'. That is, at all times, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.
